courses.course_title: The Human Planet: Prehistory to Present
courses.course_outline: This course introduces the study of archaeology and the study of human origins. Archaeology is defined as the study of the human past through material culture. Over the second semester, this course presents an overview of prehistory leading up to the modern day, as well as the broader climatic, geographic, ecological, social, and cultural contexts which made modern humans. It looks at the past through the lens of what people leave behind — the archaeological record. Students develop a broad understanding of the timescales of human prehistory and our interactions with the Earth System, from the Early Pleistocene through to the Historical period and the emergence of present day societies (the 'Anthropocene'). Students also gain an understanding of current concepts in archaeological thought and familiarity with foundational skills and terminology for present-day archaeological research. This broad course is suitable for all undergraduate students in the sciences and humanities, particularly for those wishing to major in Archaeology, Environmental & Geographical Science, or cognate subjects.
courses.dp_requirements: Attendance at lectures and tutorials and completion of assignments.
courses.assessment: Assignments and class tests count 50% towards the final mark; one 3-hour examination written in November counts 50%. A sub-minimum of 40% is required for the examination.

courses.course_title: Towards a Decolonised Science in South Africa
courses.semester: Winter term (L suffix); may not be offered every year
courses.course_outline: This course addresses key topics relevant to the decolonization of science in South Africa. Course objectives include: (1) contrasting the science behind human diversity (e.g. skin colour variation, sex) with socially-constructed categories (e.g. race, gender binary); (2) demonstrating how the history of racism and gender bias has limited the focus of scientific enquiry; (3) highlighting the importance of diversity and diverse voices in the production of scientific knowledge; (4) showing how African voices have shaped and are continuing to shape the trajectory of science. The approach is a blended learning environment combining online lectures, tutorials, tasks, and assessments. Guests from within UCT and from further afield supplement material through lectures, interviews and/or short case studies.
courses.assessment: Class participation (workshops/tutorials and online discussions) 20%; online tests 20%; final exam 30%; final project 30%. Note: The 30% final exam allocation reflects the format and intensive interactive nature of the course; the final project (equal weight to the exam) will be externally examined.

courses.prerequisites: Any first-year Science course; or any first-year Humanities course from a related discipline such as Social Anthropology, Historical Studies, Sociology; or by permission of the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: This course examines the record of primate and human evolution, showing how fossil skeletons and artefacts are interpreted in terms of human behaviour and evolutionary processes. Genetic and other comparative evidence increasingly providing insight into the origin of our lineage are also considered. Questions addressed include: Why did our ancestors evolve in Africa? How did we evolve such large and complex brains? What advantage does bipedalism provide? When do humans begin to make tools? Why is human skin colour so variable? What makes humans unique? The syllabus includes practical sessions for the study of primate and human, fossil and recent skeletal material and the artefacts associated with our ancestors.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Thursday, 3rd period. Practicals: One 2-hour practical per week, at times to be arranged.
courses.dp_requirements: Attendance at lectures and practicals and completion of assignments.
courses.assessment: Assignments and tests count 50%; one 3-hour examination in October/November counts 50%. A sub-minimum of 40% is required for the examination.

courses.prerequisites: Any first-year Science course; or any one of AXL1400F (was SAN1015F), AGE1002S or equivalent first-year semester; or AGE2011S; or any first-year Humanities course from cognate disciplines such as Anthropology, Historical Studies, Sociology; or by permission of the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: All humans living today have a common African origin. The first humans were hunter-gatherers, as were their descendants — our ancestors were hunter-gatherers for at least 99% of our evolutionary history. We learn about the origin and evolution of our hunter-gatherer ancestors from genetic, fossil, archaeological and ethnographic evidence. Studies of Khoesan peoples of southern Africa have contributed significantly to our understanding of such societies. The course focuses on the hunter-gatherer way of life over the past few hundreds of thousands of years. Specific topics include modern human origins, the Middle and Later Stone Age, ethnographic studies of Khoesan, the origins of pastoralism, coastal vs. arid environment adaptations, rock art and symbolic interpretation, genetics and biology, revisionism, and contemporary socio-politics and identity. Weekly practical sessions involve hands-on, problem-solving exercises with archaeological materials.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Thursday, 3rd period. Practicals: One 2-hour practical per week, at times to be arranged.
courses.dp_requirements: Attendance at lectures and practicals, completion of assignments and participation in a one-day museum trip.
courses.assessment: Assignments and practical tests count 50% towards the final mark; one 3-hour examination written in June counts 50%. A sub-minimum of 40% is required for the examination.

courses.prerequisites: Only for students specialising in Archaeology, with permission of the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: A flexible intensive study course in a specific area customised to the needs of individual students.
courses.assessment: Assignments count 20%; a long paper counts 40%; one 3-hour examination in November counts 40%.

courses.course_title: The Roots of Recent African Identities
courses.prerequisites: AGE2011S or AGE2012F, or by permission of the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: This course explores the history of Africa's people over the past 2000 years with special reference to southern Africa. Topics addressed include: Why are southern African populations so diverse? What lies behind the linguistic map we see today? What social, technological and palaeoenvironmental systems shaped the evolution of societies? Did Africa have any civilisations? Who did Africa interact with? The course discusses the archaeological record of artefacts, settlement systems, food waste, environmental contexts and human skeletons, deploying historical, material science, molecular science, anthropological and palaeoclimatic techniques to explore the heritage of the last two thousand years.
courses.lecture_times: Monday - Thursday, 4th period. Practicals: One 2-hour practical per week, at times to be arranged.
courses.dp_requirements: Attendance at lectures and practicals, completion of assignments.
courses.assessment: Assignments and class tests count 50% towards the final mark; one 3-hour examination written in June counts 50%. A sub-minimum of 40% is required for the examination.

courses.course_title: Archaeologies of Dominance and Resistance in the Last-500 Years
courses.prerequisites: AGE2011S or AGE2012F, or by permission of the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: The period of the last-500 years greatly impacted the social lives and economies of indigenous communities in southern Africa. Preceding this period, the importance of the African continent and its products had led to increased movements and travel from both the East and the West. From the 16th century, these movements and trade routes led to the trade in African people and increased interest in the exploitation of resources and labour on the continent. This period of interaction, domination and colonisation led to new orders of power, control and trade, greatly impacting indigenous societies subjected to slavery, genocide and eventually apartheid. The course explores the impact of these interactions, the subsequent influence and impact of the creation of a European diaspora in southern Africa, and the concept of frontier as a central theme impacting racial, gender and social relations. It also explores indigenous agency and resistance, drawing on archaeological evidence, written sources and oral history to understand the multiple lived experiences and perspectives of the period.
courses.lecture_times: Tuesday - Thursday, 5th period. Tutorials: Monday, 5th period. Practicals: Friday, 5th period.
courses.dp_requirements: Attendance at lectures and practicals, completion of assignments.
courses.assessment: Assignments and class tests count 50% towards the final mark; one 3-hour examination written in November counts 50%. A sub-minimum of 40% is required for the examination.

courses.prerequisites: Students majoring in Archaeology, or by permission of the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: The course runs throughout the academic year. The lecture programme (campus and field) is flexible and a schedule is decided upon in consultation with participating students. The curriculum covers training in site location, excavation, field note taking, stratigraphic observation, site survey, use of GPS and total station, photography, rock art recording, processing of field observations, spreadsheet use, preliminary conservation and accessioning of materials, preliminary analyses and report writing.
courses.lecture_times: See departmental notice board. Field excursions required during first (March/April) and second (June/July) vacations, including a four-week residential field-school during the second vacation. Mandatory full participation in all field excursions.
courses.dp_requirements: Participation in all field excursions and completion of all assignments.
courses.assessment: Assignments 30%; research paper 20%; practical examination 25%; theory examination 25%.

courses.prerequisites: A Bachelor's degree majoring in Archaeology and an acceptable academic record. Students must satisfy the Head of Department that they have adequate fieldwork experience.
courses.course_outline: The Honours programme in Archaeology looks in depth at current issues in the discipline, both internationally and in southern Africa. Students are expected to become fully involved in the academic life of the Department, attending seminars given by staff members, research internal students and visitors. Students participate in a structured programme of lectures and tutorials and write a research dissertation. Each student must prepare a project proposal, worked out with a supervisor and approved by the Head of Department. Students must take part in one open seminar, presenting their project to the Department, and all students are required to participate in two weeks of fieldwork.
courses.notes: Course code AGE4000W carries no NQF credit value itself. Students are concurrently registered for AGE4003W (coursework component, 112 NQF credits) and AGE4004W (research project, 48 NQF credits).
courses.assessment: On average the coursework component counts 70% (including 50% from final examinations) and the research project counts 30%. A sub-minimum of 50% is required for the research project. Coursework and project codes must each be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.prerequisites: A BSc degree with majors in both Archaeology and Environmental & Geographical Science. Acceptance at the discretion of the Head of Department.
courses.course_outline: Using the resources of both the Departments of Archaeology and Environmental & Geographical Science, this Honours programme focuses on the palaeoenvironmental context in which humans lived during the long course of the Quaternary. Course requirements include modules from both Archaeology and from Environmental & Geographical Science and a research project (48 credits).
courses.notes: Course code AGE4001W carries no NQF credit value itself. Students are concurrently registered for AGE4005W (coursework component, 112 NQF credits) and AGE4006W (research project, 48 NQF credits).
courses.assessment: On average the coursework component counts 70% (including 50% from final examinations) and the research project counts 30%. A sub-minimum of 50% is required for the research project. Coursework and project codes must each be passed separately for the award of the degree.

courses.course_outline: This course consists of an investigation of an approved topic chosen for intensive study by the candidate, culminating in the submission of a dissertation. The dissertation shall demonstrate the successful completion of a programme of training in research methods, a thorough understanding of the scientific principles underlying the research and an appropriate acquaintance with the relevant literature. It must be clearly presented and conform to the standards of the department and faculty. The dissertation will usually consist of a report detailing the conduct and analysis of the results of research performed under the close guidance of a suitably qualified supervisor/s. It should be well-conceived, acknowledge earlier research in the field, and demonstrate the ability to undertake a substantial and informed piece of research, and to collect, organise and analyse material. See also AGE5006W, Faculty of Humanities Handbook.

The PhD is a research degree on an advanced topic under supervision. The thesis must constitute a substantial contribution to knowledge in the chosen subject, must show evidence of original investigation and give a full statement of the literature on the subject. The PhD degree demands that the candidate is able to conduct independent research on their own initiative. Through the thesis the candidate must demonstrate that they are at the academic forefront in the topic selected, that the work is original and that it advances knowledge in the relevant field. Candidates are referred to the rules for this degree as set out in Book 3, General Rules and Policies.
